mappings.rationaleAI: Photographic origins through daguerreotype and Fox Talbot, photographic theory and critical frameworks (D.1), equipment and techniques from 19th century to digital era (D.2), and major genres including landscape (Ansel Adams), documentary/photojournalism (Robert Capa, Cartier-Bresson) covering both subject-determined (D.3.a) and functional photography such as photojournalism (D.3.b).

Broad survey of painting development from International Gothic and Renaissance through Impressionism and contemporary art (B.6.a), including non-Western traditions challenging Western-centric narratives (B.6.b). Covers methods of interpreting and presenting painting (B) and diverse kinds of painting across historical periods (B.5).

Philosophical examination of beauty, taste, significant form, and institutional definitions of art relate to elements of design and composition in painting (B.1). Discussion of video art and new media connects to the nature and problems of photography as art (D.1).

Survey of modern painting from Manet through Picasso, Van Gogh, Mondrian, and Young British Artists covering the history of Western painting (B.6.a). Addresses nonrepresentational painting through abstract movements (B.5.b.ii) and the institutional and critical frameworks of modernism (B).

Global contemporary art scene including the shift beyond Euro-American dominance to new regions such as China encompasses both Western painting history (B.6.a) and non-Western painting traditions (B.6.b). Art market dynamics and museum branding address the institutional context of painting today.

Renaissance painting covering composition and perspective (B.1), iconographic programs (B.2), tempera and oil media (B.3), mural painting in churches and civic spaces (B.5.a.i), and easel and manuscript painting (B.5.a.ii). Representational devotional and portrait painting (B.5.b.i) and history of Western painting (B.6.a). Drawing by Leonardo and Durer (A.4).

Surrealist and Dada painting by De Chirico, Magritte, and Miro spanning Western painting history (B.6.a) including nonrepresentational approaches (B.5.b.ii). Collage and found object practices connect to printmaking's originality questions (C.1). Photography and film as artistic media relate to photography as art (D).
Avant-garde painting movements from Seurat through Picasso and institutional contexts such as the Salon des Refuses contribute to the history of Western painting (B.6.a).

Egyptian wall painting and tomb decoration spanning four thousand years represents fixed-surface painting traditions (B.5.a.i) and the painting of non-Western peoples (B.6.b). Drawing traditions in Egyptian art connect to the history of drawing (A.4).

Christian devotional iconography of crucifixion, Virgin Mary, and saints constitutes a major iconographic tradition in painting (B.2). Frescoes such as St Francis cycles are mural painting (B.5.a.i), while Memling and Holbein panel works are easel painting (B.5.a.ii). Representational devotional painting (B.5.b.i) within Western painting history (B.6.a).
Covers letterforms, fonts, and printing design, directly relevant to printmaking media and the relationship between type and graphic arts (C).

Italian Futurist painting embracing speed, technology, and new visual aesthetics represents an important movement in Western painting history (B.6.a) with pioneering nonrepresentational approaches (B.5.b.ii).
Prehistoric cave painting at sites like Lascaux is the earliest form of painting on fixed objects (B.5.a.i) and primitive painting (B.5.c).
Romanticism as a pan-European movement encompassing painting alongside literature and music addresses painting's role in the Romantic era within the history of Western painting (B.6.a).
Development of writing systems and the relationship between script and visual representation connects to cartography and mapping as a kind of drawing determined by special uses (A.3.b.iii).
Journalism's relationship to photojournalism and documentary photography connects to functional photography including photojournalism and photo reportage (D.3.b).
